Correct_Answer: Source: Bishop - PRML (Ch 9); Murphy - MLPP (Ch 11). Setup: observed X, latent Z, params theta, log p(X|theta) = log sum_Z p(X,Z|theta). For any distribution q(Z): log p(X|theta) = L(q,theta) + KL(q || p(Z|X,theta)), where L(q,theta) = sum_Z q(Z) log[p(X,Z|theta)/q(Z)] is the ELBO and KL >= 0. Hence ELBO is a lower bound on the log-likelihood, tight iff q = p(Z|X,theta). EM: E-step - hold theta_old, maximize L over q by setting q(Z) = p(Z|X,theta_old) (makes KL=0, bound tight, ELBO = log-likelihood at theta_old). M-step - maximize L(q,theta) over theta with q fixed; this equals maximizing Q(theta,theta_old) = E_{Z~p(Z|X,theta_old)}[log p(X,Z|theta)]. Monotonicity proof: log p(X|theta_new) >= L(q,theta_new) >= L(q,theta_old) = log p(X|theta_old). First inequality: ELBO is always a lower bound. Second: M-step maximizes L over theta. Equality at end: E-step made the bound tight at theta_old. So log-likelihood never decreases (converges to a stationary point). GMM specialization (K components, means mu_k, covariances Sigma_k, mixing pi_k): E-step responsibilities gamma_{ik} = pi_k N(x_i|mu_k,Sigma_k) / sum_j pi_j N(x_i|mu_j,Sigma_j). M-step with N_k = sum_i gamma_{ik}: mu_k = (1/N_k) sum_i gamma_{ik} x_i; Sigma_k = (1/N_k) sum_i gamma_{ik}(x_i-mu_k)(x_i-mu_k)^T; pi_k = N_k / N. Iterate to convergence.
Common_Incorrect_Answer_Flag: Most plausible wrong answer: claiming EM converges to the GLOBAL maximum of the likelihood. Wrong - EM only guarantees monotonic non-decrease to a LOCAL maximum or saddle/stationary point; GMM likelihood is non-convex and outcome depends on initialization. Another error: using hard assignments (k-means-style 0/1 membership) in the GMM E-step instead of soft responsibilities gamma_{ik}, which is a different (degenerate) algorithm. Also wrong: forgetting that the E-step's choice q=p(Z|X,theta_old) is exactly what makes KL=0 and the bound tight.
Source: Bishop - Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning (Ch 9); Murphy - MLPP (Ch 11)

Question: Analyze the convergence of gradient descent on a smooth convex function. State the assumptions (L-smoothness, convexity, optionally mu-strong convexity), derive the O(1/t) rate for convex L-smooth functions with fixed step size 1/L, and contrast with the linear (geometric) rate under strong convexity. Why does momentum (Nesterov) improve the rate?
Correct_Answer: Source: Boyd & Vandenberghe - Convex Optimization; Bubeck - Convex Optimization: Algorithms and Complexity; Goodfellow et al. Ch 8. Assumptions: f convex and L-smooth (gradient is L-Lipschitz: ||grad f(x)-grad f(y)|| <= L||x-y||), which implies the descent lemma f(y) <= f(x) + grad f(x)^T(y-x) + (L/2)||y-x||^2. GD update x_{t+1} = x_t - (1/L) grad f(x_t). Using the descent lemma with step 1/L gives f(x_{t+1}) <= f(x_t) - (1/(2L))||grad f(x_t)||^2 (guaranteed decrease). Convex case: combining convexity bound f(x_t)-f* <= grad f(x_t)^T(x_t - x*) with the per-step decrease and telescoping yields f(x_t) - f* <= L||x_0 - x*||^2 / (2t) = O(1/t) sublinear rate. To reach eps suboptimality needs O(1/eps) iterations. Strongly convex case (f also mu-strongly convex, condition number kappa=L/mu): GD with step 1/L gives f(x_t)-f* <= (1 - 1/kappa)^t * C, a LINEAR / geometric rate, needing O(kappa log(1/eps)) iterations - exponentially fewer. Why Nesterov momentum helps: plain GD's O(1/t) (convex) and (1-1/kappa)^t (strongly convex) are not optimal among first-order methods. Nesterov's accelerated gradient uses a look-ahead extrapolation step y_t = x_t + beta(x_t - x_{t-1}) and achieves O(1/t^2) for convex and (1 - 1/sqrt(kappa))^t for strongly convex - matching the lower bound for first-order methods. Intuitively, momentum accumulates a velocity that damps oscillation across high-curvature directions and accelerates along low-curvature directions, reducing the effective dependence on kappa to sqrt(kappa).
Common_Incorrect_Answer_Flag: Common wrong answer: claiming plain gradient descent on a smooth convex function converges at O(1/t^2) (that is Nesterov's accelerated rate, not vanilla GD which is O(1/t)), or claiming GD converges linearly without strong convexity (linear rate requires strong convexity / PL condition). Another error: using a fixed step larger than 2/L and asserting convergence - the step must satisfy <= 1/L (or <2/L) for the descent guarantee; too large diverges. Also confusing condition number dependence: GD is O(kappa), accelerated is O(sqrt(kappa)).
Source: Boyd & Vandenberghe - Convex Optimization; Bubeck - Convex Optimization: Algorithms and Complexity

Question: Scan the opening line of Shakespeare's Sonnet 18, "Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?", marking stressed and unstressed syllables, name the prevailing metre, identify any metrical variation in the line, and explain how the rhythmic handling supports the rhetorical force of the question.
Correct_Answer: The line is iambic pentameter: five feet, each ideally an unstressed-stressed (x /) pair. Scanned: "Shall I | com-PARE | thee TO | a SUM | mer's DAY" -> x / | x / | x / | x / | x /. M.H. Abrams (A Glossary of Literary Terms) defines iambic pentameter as the staple line of the English sonnet. Strictly all five feet are regular iambs, giving a smooth, balanced ten-syllable line; however many prosodists note a possible promotion/relative stress on the opening 'Shall' (a trochaic inversion or at least a hovering stress) because the interrogative impulse pushes weight onto the first word, producing the conversational lift typical of a genuine question. The metrical regularity (no spondee, no extra syllable) makes the line feel poised and reasonable, so the hyperbolic comparison that follows is delivered as calm, measured argument rather than effusion; the rising iambic movement also mimics the rising intonation of a yes/no question. The rhetorical figure framing the whole is the opening rhetorical question (erotema), which the rest of the poem proceeds to answer and ultimately reject ('Thou art more lovely and more temperate').
Common_Incorrect_Answer_Flag: A common error is to call this trochaic, or to scan it as nine/eleven syllables, or to claim a spondee on 'SUM-MER'S'. It is iambic, decasyllabic; 'summer's' is one stressed plus one unstressed syllable within a normal iamb-iamb sequence, not a spondee. Another error is naming the opening device a 'simile' (the comparison is proposed but the poem's controlling device here is the rhetorical question; the simile is entertained then negated).
Source: M.H. Abrams - A Glossary of Literary Terms; The Norton Anthology of English Literature; Paul Fussell - Poetic Meter and Poetic Form

Question: In I.A. Richards's terminology, analyse the metaphor in "the road was a ribbon of moonlight" (Alfred Noyes, 'The Highwayman'): identify the tenor, the vehicle, and the ground, and explain why distinguishing them matters for interpretation.
Correct_Answer: I.A. Richards (The Philosophy of Rhetoric, 1936) split a metaphor into the tenor (the underlying subject actually meant), the vehicle (the figure used to convey it), and the ground (the shared features that make the transfer apt). Here the tenor is 'the road'; the vehicle is 'a ribbon of moonlight'; the ground is the set of resemblances: the road's long, thin, sinuous, pale, gleaming quality under the moon, evoking delicacy and beauty. Distinguishing them matters because meaning arises from the interaction (Richards's 'interaction view') between tenor and vehicle, not from the vehicle alone; the connotations of 'ribbon' (fragile, ornamental, gift-like) and 'moonlight' (romantic, transient, spectral) colour our sense of the road and foreshadow the poem's doomed romance. Misreading which is tenor and which is vehicle inverts the whole image.
Common_Incorrect_Answer_Flag: The most plausible error is reversing tenor and vehicle - treating 'ribbon of moonlight' as the literal subject and 'road' as the figure - or conflating vehicle with ground. The vehicle is the borrowed image (ribbon of moonlight); the ground is the basis of comparison (long, pale, gleaming, slender), not itself a part of the sentence. Calling the whole thing a 'simile' is also wrong: there is no 'like'/'as'; it is an implicit/copula metaphor.
Source: I.A. Richards - The Philosophy of Rhetoric; M.H. Abrams - A Glossary of Literary Terms

Question: Take the line "This is just to say / I have eaten / the plums" (William Carlos Williams). Contrast how a New Critic, a reader-response critic (Iser/Fish), and a deconstructionist (Derrida) would each account for the poem's meaning, and explain the central methodological disagreement among them.
Correct_Answer: New Criticism (Brooks, Wimsatt) would treat the poem as a self-contained verbal artefact, reading it via close reading of tension, irony and the apparent gap between the apologetic frame ('Forgive me') and the sensuous, unrepentant relish ('so sweet / and so cold'); meaning is in the text's structure, and appeals to author's intention (the 'intentional fallacy') or to reader's feelings (the 'affective fallacy') are excluded. Reader-response (Wolfgang Iser's gaps/Leerstellen; Stanley Fish's interpretive communities) locates meaning in the reader's act of concretising the note: the poem's terseness leaves blanks the reader fills (Was it a real apology? Whose plums?), and for Fish the very recognition of it AS a poem is produced by an interpretive community's conventions. Deconstruction (Derrida) would foreground the undecidability: the 'apology' both apologises and refuses to apologise, the title 'This is just to say' folds the everyday note into 'poem' so that genre, sincerity, and reference become aporetic; differance defers any settled meaning, and the binary apology/transgression is shown to be unstable, each term inhabiting the other. The central disagreement is over the LOCUS and STABILITY of meaning: New Criticism = stable meaning IN the autonomous text; reader-response = meaning produced BY the reader/community; deconstruction = meaning perpetually deferred and self-subverting, with no stable centre in text or reader. Terry Eagleton (Literary Theory: An Introduction) frames exactly this migration of meaning from text to reader to the play of language.
Common_Incorrect_Answer_Flag: A frequent error is to collapse reader-response into deconstruction (both 'subjective'), or to attribute the 'intentional fallacy' to deconstruction rather than to New Criticism (it is Wimsatt & Beardsley's). Another is treating New Criticism as concerned with historical/biographical context - it is precisely anti-contextual, focusing on the words on the page. Saying deconstruction 'finds the hidden true meaning' is wrong: it denies any single recoverable meaning.
Source: Terry Eagleton - Literary Theory: An Introduction; Wimsatt & Beardsley - The Intentional Fallacy / The Affective Fallacy; Wolfgang Iser - The Act of Reading; Stanley Fish - Is There a Text in This Class?

Question: The sentence "The professor said on Monday she would resign" is structurally ambiguous. Diagnose the ambiguity in syntactic terms (attachment of the adverbial), give the two paraphrases, and explain the principle that usually governs default interpretation.
Correct_Answer: The ambiguity is one of adverbial PP attachment: the time adjunct 'on Monday' can attach (a) high, to the matrix clause headed by 'said' [The professor, on Monday, said that she would resign], or (b) low, into the embedded complement clause headed by 'resign' [The professor said that, on Monday, she would resign]. In a tree, reading (a) adjoins 'on Monday' to the VP of the main clause (modifying 'said'); reading (b) adjoins it to the VP inside the subordinate that-clause (modifying 'would resign'). The two paraphrases: (a) The speaking happened on Monday; (b) The resigning is scheduled for Monday. Quirk & Greenbaum (A Comprehensive Grammar of the English Language) treat this as a scope/attachment ambiguity of adjuncts. Parsing preference principles such as Late Closure / Right Association (Frazier; Kimball) predict the processor by default attaches the new constituent to the most recently built phrase, favouring the LOW (b) reading - 'resign on Monday' - though Minimal Attachment and real-world plausibility can override this.
Common_Incorrect_Answer_Flag: A common wrong answer labels this a lexical ambiguity or 'just a comma problem,' or claims only one reading exists. It is structural (syntactic attachment), not lexical. Another error: identifying 'she' as the source of ambiguity (pronoun reference) - while 'she' could in principle refer to someone else, the question's intended ambiguity is the adverbial attachment. Misnaming Late Closure as 'Minimal Attachment' is also a frequent slip; Minimal Attachment minimises nodes, Late Closure attaches to the current phrase.
Source: Quirk & Greenbaum - A Comprehensive Grammar of the English Language; L. Frazier & J. Fodor - parsing strategies (Late Closure / Minimal Attachment)

Question: Scan Tennyson's "Half a league, half a league, / Half a league onward" (The Charge of the Light Brigade), name the metre, and explain how the chosen foot and the use of a catalectic line enact the poem's content.
Correct_Answer: The lines are predominantly DACTYLIC dimeter. Scansion: 'HALF a league, | HALF a league' -> / x x | / x x (two dactyls); 'HALF a league | ON-ward' -> / x x | / x (a dactyl followed by a catalectic foot - the final dactyl is docked of its last unstressed syllable, i.e. catalexis). The falling rhythm of the dactyl (/ x x) mimics the relentless, galloping hoofbeats of the cavalry charge, the repetition driving urgency and inevitability; the truncated/catalectic close on 'ONward' lands hard on a stress, giving a clipped, forward-thrusting finality that pushes the riders 'onward' into the valley. Abrams and Fussell note dactylic metre's association with martial momentum. The insistent anaphora ('Half a league, half a league, half a league') reinforces the mechanical, doomed forward motion ('into the valley of Death').
Common_Incorrect_Answer_Flag: The most likely error is calling it anapaestic (x x /) - that is the rising mirror image, but Tennyson's lines clearly begin on a stress ('HALF'), so they are FALLING (dactylic), not rising. Another error: calling 'onward' a trochee in a trochaic line - the governing foot is the dactyl; 'onward' is a catalectic (truncated) dactyl, not evidence of trochaic metre. Naming the metre 'iambic' is plainly wrong given the stress-initial pattern.
Source: M.H. Abrams - A Glossary of Literary Terms; Paul Fussell - Poetic Meter and Poetic Form; The Norton Anthology of English Literature

Question: A asks "Is the new manager any good?" and B replies "Well, she's always punctual." Using Grice's Cooperative Principle, identify the maxim B appears to flout, derive the conversational implicature, and distinguish this from entailment and presupposition.
Correct_Answer: Grice (Logic and Conversation, in Studies in the Way of Words) posits the Cooperative Principle and its maxims (Quantity, Quality, Relation, Manner). B's reply flouts (exploits, not merely violates) the maxim of QUANTITY - and arguably Relation - by saying conspicuously less than the question requires while remaining true. Because A assumes B is still cooperating, A derives the conversational IMPLICATURE that the manager is NOT good at the substantive aspects of the job (damning with faint praise): if B could have offered something more impressive, the maxim of Quantity required her to; her retreat to a trivial virtue implicates a negative overall assessment. Crucially this is implicature, which is CANCELLABLE/defeasible (B could add 'and she's brilliant strategically too', removing it) and NON-DETACHABLE - unlike entailment, which is part of truth-conditional meaning and cannot be cancelled ('she is punctual' entails 'she arrives on time'), and unlike presupposition, which survives negation ('the new manager' presupposes there is a new manager, and would still do so under 'Is the new manager not good?'). Levinson (Pragmatics) and Yule (Pragmatics) give exactly this contrast.
Common_Incorrect_Answer_Flag: Common errors: (i) saying B 'violates' the maxim - Grice distinguishes a covert violation (intent to mislead) from an overt FLOUT that generates implicature; this is a flout. (ii) Naming the maxim of Quality (truthfulness) - B is being truthful, so Quality is observed; the flouted maxim is Quantity/Relation. (iii) Treating the inference as an entailment - it is defeasible implicature, not logically guaranteed. (iv) Confusing implicature with presupposition (presupposition is the manager's existence, projected and constant under negation).
Source: H.P. Grice - Logic and Conversation (Studies in the Way of Words); Stephen Levinson - Pragmatics; George Yule - Pragmatics

Question: Explain the grammatical difference between "If I were you" and "If I was late", classify each conditional, and account for why 'were' (not 'was') is prescribed in the first using the categories of mood. Comment on the descriptive vs prescriptive status of 'was' in the first slot.
Correct_Answer: 'If I were you' is a counterfactual (Type 2 / 'second' / unreal-present) conditional: it expresses a hypothetical contrary to fact (I am not you), and standardly takes the past SUBJUNCTIVE mood, whose only distinctive surviving form in modern English is 'were' for all persons (the 'were-subjunctive', Quirk & Greenbaum). Hence prescriptively 'If I were you'. 'If I was late' is normally an OPEN/real conditional referring to an uncertain past fact (a Type 1-style or factual/open conditional - 'if it is the case that I was late'), using the ordinary past INDICATIVE 'was'; here 'was' is correct because no counterfactuality is asserted. Mood categories: indicative (statements of fact), subjunctive (hypothetical/non-factual), imperative (commands). Descriptively, 'If I was you' is extremely common in informal speech and is increasingly accepted, since the were/was distinction in the 1st/3rd singular is eroding; prescriptive grammars (and Wren & Martin / Fowler) still require 'were' in the counterfactual. So the contrast is mood-driven AND conditional-type-driven, not a simple right/wrong of 'was' vs 'were' across the board.
Common_Incorrect_Answer_Flag: The likely error is a blanket rule 'always use were after if' - false; 'If I was late (to the meeting), I apologise' is a perfectly grammatical open/indicative conditional. The opposite error - 'were' is simply old-fashioned and always replaceable by 'was' - ignores the prescriptive counterfactual subjunctive. Also wrong: calling 'If I were you' a Type 3 (past unreal) conditional - it is Type 2 (present/future unreal); Type 3 uses past perfect ('If I had been you').
Source: Quirk & Greenbaum - A Comprehensive Grammar of the English Language; Wren & Martin - High School English Grammar and Composition; H.W. Fowler - A Dictionary of Modern English Usage

Question: Using the stylistic concepts of foregrounding, deviation and parallelism, analyse e.e. cummings's line "anyone lived in a pretty how town" and explain how the grammatical deviance produces its thematic meaning.
Correct_Answer: Stylistics (Geoffrey Leech, A Linguistic Guide to English Poetry; Leech & Short, Style in Fiction) treats FOREGROUNDING as the artistically motivated 'making strange' of language, achieved by DEVIATION (departure from linguistic norms) and PARALLELISM (unexpected regularity). In 'anyone lived in a pretty how town': (1) Lexical/grammatical deviation - the indefinite pronoun 'anyone' is forced into the slot of a proper noun / referring NP (a personal name), and the interrogative/adverbial 'how' is wedged ungrammatically into an adjectival slot ('a pretty how town'), violating English phrase-structure rules. (2) This deviance is foregrounded against the otherwise nursery-rhyme regularity of the metre. Thematically, turning 'anyone' into a character makes the protagonist Everyman/no-one in particular, expressing the poem's vision of anonymous, cyclical small-town existence and the namelessness of ordinary lives ('Women and men... cared for anyone not at all'); 'how town' suggests a place defined by routine ('how') rather than identity. The deviation thus IS the meaning - form enacting the loss of individuality. Roman Jakobson's notion of the poetic function (projecting the principle of equivalence from selection onto combination) underwrites the parallelism.
Common_Incorrect_Answer_Flag: A common error is to dismiss the line as 'just a grammatical mistake' or random nonsense, missing that the deviation is rule-governed and meaningful (foregrounding). Another is mislabelling 'anyone' and 'how' as simple metaphors or personification - the device is grammatical/syntactic deviation, not a trope of comparison. Calling the regular metre 'deviation' is also wrong: regularity is parallelism, deviation is the norm-breaking.
Source: Geoffrey Leech - A Linguistic Guide to English Poetry; Leech & Short - Style in Fiction; Roman Jakobson - Linguistics and Poetics

Question: Define the 'dissociation of sensibility' as theorised by T.S. Eliot, situate it in literary history (which poets it praises and blames), and explain the critique later levelled against the concept by Frank Kermode.
Correct_Answer: In 'The Metaphysical Poets' (1921) T.S. Eliot coined 'dissociation of sensibility' to name a supposed historical split, setting in around the mid/late 17th century, between thought and feeling - a separation of intellect from emotion in English poetry. Before it, the Metaphysical poets (Donne, Marvell, Herbert) possessed a 'unified sensibility' in which 'a thought to Donne was an experience; it modified his sensibility,' fusing reason and passion (Eliot praises their conceits and felt intelligence). After the split - which Eliot links partly to Milton and Dryden - poets either thought OR felt but not both; the Romantics (e.g. Tennyson, Browning later) 'ruminated' rather than felt their thought. Eliot thus elevates the Metaphysicals and demotes Milton/the Romantics, a valuation that reshaped 20th-century canon-formation. Frank Kermode (Romantic Image, 1957) attacked the idea as historically unfounded and ideologically motivated: he argued the 'dissociation' is a critical myth that conveniently projects the Symbolist/modernist ideal of the unified image back onto the 17th century, that no such datable cultural fall actually occurred, and that the doctrine served Eliot's own poetic-political programme rather than describing real literary history.
Common_Incorrect_Answer_Flag: Frequent errors: dating the dissociation to the Renaissance/Elizabethan period or to the 19th century rather than the 17th; reversing the valuation (thinking Eliot praised Milton and disparaged the Metaphysicals - it is the opposite); attributing the concept to F.R. Leavis or I.A. Richards rather than Eliot. Also wrong is summarising it as a split between 'form and content' - it is between thought and feeling (intellect and sensibility).
Source: T.S. Eliot - The Metaphysical Poets (Selected Essays); Frank Kermode - Romantic Image; M.H. Abrams - A Glossary of Literary Terms

Question: Close-read the final couplet of Robert Frost's 'Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening' - "And miles to go before I sleep, / And miles to go before I sleep." Using William Empson's notion of ambiguity, explain the effect of the exact repetition, identify the relevant type of ambiguity, and connect the metrical/phonic features to the theme.
Correct_Answer: The two lines are identical iambic tetrameter ('and MILES | to GO | be-FORE | I SLEEP' -> x / x / x / x /), part of the poem's interlocking rubaiyat-derived rhyme scheme (aaba, bbcb...) which here closes with a quatrain whose repetition substitutes for further rhyme-linkage, sealing the poem. William Empson (Seven Types of Ambiguity, 1930) shows how a single expression can carry multiple simultaneous meanings; the repeated 'miles to go before I sleep' exploits the polysemy of 'sleep': on first utterance it reads literally - the speaker has a long journey before bedtime; on the verbatim repetition the SAME words acquire a figurative weight, 'sleep' shading into death and 'miles to go' into the duties/years of a life still to be lived. This is close to Empson's ambiguity in which a word is effective in two senses at once (the literal and the symbolic coexist; arguably his Type 1 - a detail effective in several ways - tipping toward the deeper resonance). The exact verbal repetition with no syntactic change foregrounds the semantic shift, performing a movement from the physical to the metaphysical; the hushed monosyllables, the soft sibilant/liquid sounds ('sleep'), and the regular, lulling iambic beat enact the seductive pull of rest/oblivion against the 'promises' that compel the speaker onward. Theme: the tension between the allure of cessation (the lovely, dark, deep woods / sleep / death) and obligation (promises, miles to go / life's continuance).
Common_Incorrect_Answer_Flag: A common error is to insist the repetition is 'merely for emphasis' with no change of meaning - this misses the Empsonian point that identical words can shift sense in context. Another is over-literalising 'sleep' as only death (Frost himself resisted a fixed allegory; the power is the COEXISTENCE of literal and figurative, i.e. ambiguity, not a single decoded meaning). Mis-scanning the line as pentameter or trochaic is also wrong: it is iambic TETRAMETER (four feet). Naming the form a sonnet or terza rima is incorrect; it is an interlocking rubai-type stanza pattern.
Source: William Empson - Seven Types of Ambiguity; M.H. Abrams - A Glossary of Literary Terms; The Norton Anthology of English Literature
